region_of_origin: India (North Indian / Mughlai), served in Singapore

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Murgh Malai Kebab; closest match is chicken kebab food product (FOODON_03311349).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Murgh Malai Kebab; A0CKN (Chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for this marinated and grilled chicken preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Murgh Malai Kebab or a sufficiently close prepared chicken dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Murgh Malai Kebab is approximately 200 g, equivalent to 4–5 skewered chicken pieces, as commonly served at Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Murgh Malai Kebab — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient record (G00010252); supplemented by USDA FoodData Central Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted (FDC ID 171477) for fatty acid fractions and vitamin C
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values — energy (302 kcal), protein (10.7 g), fat (26.4 g), carbohydrate (5.24 g), sugar (2.85 g), sodium (1080 mg), dietary fibre (0.0 g), potassium (387 mg), calcium (118 mg), magnesium (12 mg), iron (1.15 mg), and cholesterol (85 mg) — are taken directly from the HPB Foodo record for Murgh Malai Kebab (cr_id G00010252, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (9.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.25 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.5 mg/100g) are not reported in the HPB record; these values are estimated by adaptation from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) adjusted for the cream and butter marinade contribution typical of Murgh Malai Kebab, and are consistent with the values present in the input record. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not sufficiently reported for this specific preparation in either source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate for marinated grilled chicken with cream-based dairy marinade
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Malai Kebab in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI is estimated at 35 based on the closest analogous food category documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283): protein-dominant, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked chicken preparations with dairy-based marinades consistently yield GI values in the 30–40 range. Net carbohydrate content is 5.24 g/100g. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (35 × 5.24 × 2.00) / 100 = 3.67, rounded to 3.7. This is classified as low GL (< 10). The estimation basis is fully documented and grounded in a peer-reviewed approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1080 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit Murgh Malai Kebab due to its very high sodium content of 1080 mg/100g; if consumed, request reduced-salt preparation, avoid additional sauces or chutneys, and limit portion to no more than 100 g to keep sodium contribution below 1100 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g) and low estimated GI (~35) support glycaemic stability, but high fat (26.4 g/100g) may delay gastric emptying and affect post-meal glucose response variability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Malai Kebab is relatively low in carbohydrate and has a low estimated glycaemic index, making it a reasonable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high fat content (26.4 g/100g, including 9.0 g saturated fat) should be monitored for its effect on insulin sensitivity and post-meal glucose response, and sweet chutneys or dipping sauces must be avoided.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.0 g/100g and sodium 1080 mg/100g both exceed cardiovascular-safe thresholds; trans fat 0.25 g/100g adds additional atherogenic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Murgh Malai Kebab only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high saturated fat (9.0 g/100g from cream and butter/ghee), trans fat (0.25 g/100g), and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) presents a compounded risk for arterial stiffness, LDL elevation, and blood pressure elevation; substituting cream with low-fat yogurt and requesting salt reduction would meaningfully lower the cardiovascular risk profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 9.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold; butter and ghee are explicitly pro-inflammatory and do not qualify for the MUFA/PUFA carve-out; sodium 1080 mg/100g worsens RA-related tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Murgh Malai Kebab is not recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to high saturated fat from butter/ghee and cream (9.0 g/100g), which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and very high sodium (1080 mg/100g) that exacerbates joint swelling; green chillies (nightshade vegetables) may also trigger individual flare responses and should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Malai Kebab (Hindi: मुर्ग मलाई कबाब) is a classic North Indian and Mughlai dish consisting of boneless chicken pieces marinated in a rich, creamy mixture of fresh cream (malai), cheese (paneer or processed cheese), yogurt, butter or ghee, garlic, ginger, green chilli, kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves), cardamom, white pepper, and lemon juice. The marinated chicken is traditionally cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) or on a charcoal grill, producing a soft, succulent, mildly spiced kebab with a pale golden exterior and a distinctly creamy, buttery flavour profile. Unlike red-spiced tandoori preparations, Murgh Malai Kebab is characterised by its white or pale yellow colour, delicate spicing, and melt-in-the-mouth texture derived from the high dairy fat content. It is widely served in Indian restaurants across Singapore as a starter or main course, typically accompanied by mint chutney, sliced onions, and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, it is high in fat (26.4 g/100g, including 9.0 g saturated fat from cream and butter), moderate in protein (10.7 g/100g), and low in carbohydrate (5.24 g/100g), yielding 302 kcal/100g. Sodium is very high at 1080 mg/100g, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The low carbohydrate content and estimated low glycaemic index (~35) make it a reasonable protein source for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, provided sweet accompaniments are avoided. Kasuri methi provides evidence-based hypoglycaemic properties. The dish is halal and gluten-free.

ingredient_synonyms.tandoor: traditional clay oven used in South Asian cooking

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, and cholesterol) are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence for an exact named record (G00010252). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) with adjustment for the cream and butter marinade contribution, consistent with the input record values. GI is an analogous estimate grounded in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, peer-reviewed). Overall confidence is high given the primary HPB source coverage of all core macros and approved secondary source coverage of remaining nutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Murgh Malai Kebab is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the specificity required for this preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this preparation is not reported in the HPB Foodo record or any other approved source consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo record for this dish, and no approved secondary source provides sufficient specificity for this preparation. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is plausible given the low-fibre marinade ingredients (cream, cheese, butter, spices) and the absence of vegetable components in the kebab itself. 5. Saturated fat (9.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.25 g/100g), and vitamin C (1.5 mg/100g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171477 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted) with adjustment for the cream and butter marinade contribution typical of Murgh Malai Kebab; these values are consistent with the input record. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for Murgh Malai Kebab exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature.
